spot_checks_passed.verified: 10^9 storage-capacity prediction correctly attributed to Turing's own conjecture at the start of Section 6. PDF reads: 'I believe that in about fifty years' time it will be possible, to programme computers, with a storage capacity of about 109, to make them play the imitation game so well that an average interrogator will not have more than 70 per cent chance of making the right identification after five minutes of questioning.' Extraction's l3_json correctly notes the OCR artifact ('109' = 10^9) and the 70% / 5-minute parameters. verification_status='proposed_in_paper' is appropriate (Turing frames as belief/prediction, not result).

Brain storage estimate of 10^10 to 10^15 binary digits and the 10^9 'satisfactory imitation-game' figure correctly attributed to Section 7 (Learning Machines), and the Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th-ed. comparison value 2 x 10^9 is verbatim in the PDF. The 10^9 figure here is for the imitation-game storage estimate ('against a blind man'); not conflated with the rel1 prediction. Page attribution to p.455 matches PDF body text just before the section on 'finding how to programme these machines to play the game'.

Lady Lovelace objection correctly captured. PDF: 'Our most detailed information of Babbage's Analytical Engine comes from a memoir by Lady Lovelace (1842). In it she states, "The Analytical Engine has no pretensions to originate anything. It can do whatever we know how to order it to perform" (her italics). This statement is quoted by Hartree (1949) who adds: "This does not imply that it may not be possible to construct electronic equipment which will think for itself..."' Extraction faithfully captures both the Lovelace quote and Turing's response (universal computer can in principle have the property; reduces to learning-machine question). source_section '(6) Lady Lovelace's Objection' matches the PDF subsection heading.

Argument-from-Consciousness response correctly attributed to objection (4). PDF source_section is 'The Argument from Consciousness'. The Jefferson Lister Oration 1949 quote ('Not until a machine can write a sonnet or compose a concerto...') is correctly identified in paper_references[6] as 'Professor Jefferson's Lister Oration for 1949'. The extraction's source_text quoting the solipsism reduction ('the only way by which one could be sure that machine thinks is to be the machine ... It is in fact the solipsist point of view') is verbatim from PDF p.446. The summary correctly notes Jefferson's view does not collapse to extreme solipsism — Turing himself says 'I am sure that Professor Jefferson does not wish to adopt the extreme and solipsist point of view'.

design_basis='theoretical' is correct: this is a philosophical/conceptual paper without empirical sampling. 'philosophical_argumentation_with_thought_experiments' study_design and author_framing='suggestive' are also appropriate, since Turing explicitly writes 'I have no very convincing arguments of a positive nature to support my views.' No empirical-design overclaim.

Storage capacity definition (log base 2 of number of states; Manchester ~165,000; wheel example ~1.6; capacities of combined machines add) verified against Section 5 of the PDF. Distinguished from raw count of states / physical bytes correctly captured. No conflation with the 10^9 prediction figure.

Manchester 1,000-storage-unit unpredictability demonstration correctly attributed to objection (8) Argument from Informality of Behaviour. PDF: 'I have set up on the Manchester computer a small programme using only 1,000 units of storage, whereby the machine supplied with one sixteen-figure number replies with another within two seconds. I would defy anyone to learn from these replies sufficient about the programme to be able to predict any replies to untried values.' Verification_status='verified_in_paper' with design_basis='computational_demonstration' is appropriate since Turing reports having actually set this up.
